mappings.rationale: Directly covers human heredity, genome, variation, and race -- the central topics of this section (A, B).
Explains cell division, chromosomes, mutations, cystic fibrosis inheritance, and sex linkage, directly relevant to genetic reproduction and chromosome variations (A.1, A.2.b).
Introduces DNA sequencing and genomic technologies that underpin our understanding of human genetic variation and applied human genetics (A.3, B.1).
Details DNA structure, Mendelian inheritance patterns, and molecular mechanisms underlying genetic reproduction and chromosome variations (A.1, A.2.b).
Illuminates how identical twins form and how cell differentiation translates genotype into phenotypic variation (A.2.a, B.1).
Ancient DNA analysis and hominin speciation bear directly on the nature and origin of human physical variation (B).
Explains genetic drift, natural selection, and population-level mechanisms that generate human physical diversity (B.1, B.2).
Examines IQ measurement, Wechsler scales, and twin studies on cognitive heritability, directly relevant to inheritance of behavioral traits (A.2, A.4).
Traces historical applications of human genetics to population control and racial classification, from Francis Galton's founding ideas onward (A.3, B.2).
Directly addresses critiques of old racial measures such as colour, hair form, and body measurements, and institutional racism (B.2).
Provides cultural perspectives on human diversity and the anthropological critique of biological race concepts (B.1, B.2).
Engages with the nature-versus-nurture debate, essentialism, and species concepts relevant to scientific controversies about race and heredity (A.2, B.2).
Analyzes population distribution and life chances relevant to understanding patterns of human diversity across populations (B.1).
